nativeRange.habitat: Tisbe biminiensis inhabits shallow tropical and subtropical coastal waters, including coral reef flats, lagoons, mangrove sediments, and macroalgal beds. It lives epibenthically — that is, on or just above the seafloor — crawling across sand, crushed coral rubble, live rock, and macroalgae to graze on the thin films of bacteria, microalgae, and detritus that coat these surfaces.

decorPreferences: pod hotels, coarse filter floss bundles, or scrubber mat for crawl surface
gentle aeration via airstone (no fine micro-bubbles)
sponge pre-filter or no mechanical filtration in the culture
no copper or zinc surfaces in contact with culture water

diet.notes: The Tisbe Pod is a benthic grazer that has been documented eating bacterial biofilms, benthic microalgae (especially diatoms such as Thalassiosira spp.), detritus, and leftover fish food on substrate, rocks, and tank panels. In culture, it is generally advised to feed live phytoplankton — Nannochloropsis, Isochrysis, Tetraselmis, or commercial reef phyto blends — to supply the essential fatty acids (docosahexaenoic acid and eicosapentaenoic acid, commonly abbreviated DHA and EPA) that support egg production. This can be supplemented with inert powdered diets such as commercial copepod-culture rations. The species prefers settled benthic food over suspended particles.

goodWith: monoculture in dedicated culture container, or seeded into refugia and display reef tanks
harvested or self-recruiting prey for mandarin dragonets, scooter blennies, pipefish, seahorses, anthias, fairy wrasses, leopard wrasses, and other planktivorous reef fish
browsed by SPS, LPS, NPS corals and clams (nauplii are coral-sized prey)
coexists with most reef cleanup crew (snails, hermits, mini-brittle stars)
avoidWith: any copper-based medication (cupramine, Coppersafe, etc.) in the same system
wrasses and gobies that actively hunt pods in the refugium (six-line wrasse, melanurus wrasse, dragonets) — keep refugium predator-free
fine mechanical filtration or skimming on the culture container (removes nauplii)
chlorinated/chloraminated tap water; metal plumbing in contact with culture water

summary: The Tisbe Pod (Tisbe biminiensis) is a small benthic copepod widely cultured for reef refugia and as a live food for planktivorous fish such as mandarin dragonets and seahorses. More bottom-dwelling than Tigriopus copepods, it also grazes detritus and biofilm, making it a useful biological cleaner.
careNotes:
The Tisbe Pod is not a display animal — it is kept primarily as a refugium organism and a live food source, rather than for observation in a main display tank. Adults reach roughly 0.5-1.0 mm in body length, making them invisible to the naked eye at any distance; their nauplii (newly hatched larvae) are around 50-75 µm, small enough to be captured by coral polyps and by larval reef fish. In reef tanks, Tisbe pods colonize live rock crevices and sandbed surfaces on their own once introduced, cycling through generations without keeper intervention.

Reef-strength salinity is preferred (specific gravity approximately 1.020-1.026, equivalent to roughly 26-34 ppt). Reproduction has been reported to decline sharply below approximately 1.020 SG, and brief dips to about 1.015 SG are tolerated but not recommended for a productive culture. Temperature should be kept within the 22-29°C range, with the reproductive peak falling toward the upper end.

Tisbe biminiensis is extremely sensitive to copper-based medications, zinc, and many common aquarium chemicals — copper should never be dosed in any tank, sump, or culture vessel intended to host a Tisbe population. Avoid metal plumbing, zinc-coated hardware, and chlorinated tap water in contact with culture water.

In dedicated culture, hobbyists generally use 4-20 L bare-bottom containers with gentle aeration via an airstone, weekly partial water changes, and added structure — pod hotels, coarse filter floss bundles, or scrubber mat — to give the pods surfaces to crawl on and hide in. Sponge pre-filters or no mechanical filtration are recommended so nauplii are not removed. Under optimal conditions, cultures have been reported to exceed 67,000 individuals per liter.

Tisbe biminiensis is often paired with Tigriopus californicus (a larger, more open-water harpacticoid copepod) in commercial copepod blends, since the two species occupy complementary ecological niches — Tisbe works the substrate and rockwork while Tigriopus tends to remain more active in the water column.
breedingNotes:
The Tisbe Pod is generally regarded as one of the most prolific harpacticoid copepods in captive culture. Females reach reproductive maturity in roughly 7-10 days after their final molt and are reported to carry around 8-10 successive broods over a lifespan of approximately 3-4 weeks, with each new egg sac forming about two days after the previous brood hatches. Females carry visible paired egg sacs at the rear of the body (the urosome); the newly hatched nauplii are free-swimming and planktonic before gradually settling to a crawling, benthic lifestyle as they develop through the copepodite stages. Reproduction is sexual, with separate male and female individuals; the species does not reproduce by parthenogenesis (females reproducing without males). Under optimal conditions (around 25-29°C, salinity approximately 34 ppt / SG ~1.025, with abundant diatoms or phytoplankton), cultures have been reported to exceed 67,000 individuals per liter.

It is generally advised to maintain multiple staggered culture jars — starting a new jar from an established culture every few weeks — and to refresh the founding population every 4-6 weeks to prevent genetic bottlenecks or culture crash. To harvest, gently swirl the culture and collect the water through a fine sieve: approximately 50 µm mesh captures nauplii, while approximately 150 µm mesh retains adults and older copepodites.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Designated primary did not surface a species-specific product page for Tisbe biminiensis during this research run (homepage only). Substituted AlgaeBarn's Tisbe biminiensis blog as functional primary; URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: Reef2Reef Wiki
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 Forbidden during this research run; substituted Pod Your Reef's Tisbe biminiensis complete guide as functional secondary. URL retained for reviewer reference.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn - A Closer Look at the Harpacticoid Copepod Tisbe biminiensis
additional.notes: Functional primary substitute. Source for biology (benthic grazer, bacterial films + diatoms + detritus), brood structure (~9 broods per female, ~2 days between broods, ~7-10 days to first brood), reproductive ceiling (>67,000 individuals/L), substrate flexibility (sand, crushed coral, bare bottoms), ecological niche (epifaunal harpacticoid), and tropical/subtropical distribution.

additional.name: Pod Your Reef - Tisbe biminiensis Complete Guide
additional.notes: Functional secondary. Cross-confirmed adult size 0.5-0.75 mm (max ~1 mm) and nauplii 50-75 µm, lifespan 2-4 weeks, reef salinity 1.025-1.030 SG (down to ~20 ppt tolerance), tropical/Caribbean origin (species named for Bimini, Bahamas), and 8-10 broods per female.

additional.name: AlgaGen - Tisbe Copepods Care: Temperature & Feeding
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed temperature 22-25°C (72-78°F) optimum with reproductive peak at upper end, salinity 1.020-1.026 SG, extreme copper sensitivity, pod-hotel/floss culture structure, and feeding (live phyto + diatoms + biofilm + detritus). Also flagged risk of rapid temperature swings.

additional.name: Souza-Santos et al. 2006 - Developing the harpacticoid copepod Tisbe biminiensis culture (Aquaculture Research / Wiley)
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed aquaculture triangulation source. Mass culture at 29°C, salinity 34 g/L (SG ~1.025), 12L:12D photoperiod, water renewal every other day; salinity tolerance from 34 down to 27 g/L with reduced offspring, no tolerance at 20 g/L. Confirms 50-100% copepodite-biomass daily ration as optimal feeding rate. Also corroborates SaltwaterAquarium.com / AlgaeBarn product-page consumer list (mandarins, seahorses, anthias, wrasses, SPS/LPS/NPS corals, clams).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Harpacticoid copepods develop through six naupliar stages (N1-N6) and five copepodite stages (C1-C5) before reaching adult form (C6), shedding their exoskeleton at each transition. At 25-29°C, each stage lasts roughly 1-3 days. The shed exoskeletons are microscopic and fall into the detrital pool that the population grazes on; their presence in the culture is widely considered normal and not a sign of trouble.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction; females carry paired egg sacs that hatch into planktonic nauplii. See top-level breedingNotes for the full culture protocol.
